data.title: General Dynamics: A Rare Blend Of Stability, Attractive Dividend And Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Owing to the complex economic landscape marked by geopolitical tensions and shifting market dynamics, investors have to navigate the headwinds. As investors navigate these challenges, I find General Dynamics Corporation (NYSE:GD) to be a resilient investment opportunity. The company’s diversified portfolio, strong financial performance and market position offer an interesting opportunity for long-term investors. Perhaps, those seeking stability, growth, and income. It is against this backdrop that I rate this stock a buy.

Financial Performance & Growth Momentum

GD has exemplified consistent financial strength, underpinned by solid revenue growth and consistent profitability.

While the above chart demonstrates its resilient financial performance historically, its most recent performance cements it. In Q1 2025, the company reported $12.22 billion in revenue, 13.9% YoY growth. It was the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit revenue growth and at least the 10th successive quarter of positive revenue growth. Its EPS was $3.66 beating Wall Street estimates by about 5.7%, and growing by more than 27% YoY. Net income grew to approximately $994 million, reflecting a 23% improvement compared to the previous year.

These results depict the company’s ability to deliver double-digit growth across its key metrics. As such, it signifies the company’s strong operational execution and demand for its products and services. Given its book-to-bill ratio of 0.8x and a total contract value of more than $141 billion, this company is well positioned for sustained revenue growth.

Its diversity played a crucial role in its performance. The aerospace segment had a stand-out performance with 45.2% and 59.4% in revenue and operating earnings growth. This was driven by higher aircraft production. Further, the marine systems had its revenue growing by 7.7% to about $3.6 billion, with both technologies and combat systems segments registering 6.8% and 3.5% revenue growth, respectively.

Comparatively, its financials are not only the best amongst its peers, but also the most stable. This speaks to its resilience and consistency relative to its peers.

In general, GD continues to exhibit resilience and upward momentum in its financials, which suggests a strong demand for its offerings. The strong order backlog at $88.7 billion in Q1 2025 and the estimated contract value of more than $141 billion points to a sustainable financial growth in the future.

Positioned To Leverage Geopolitical Tensions

Around the globe, the military budget has been on the rise, driven by ongoing geopolitical differences. In 2024, the budget reaches a record high of about $2.5 trillion, marking more than 7% YoY growth.

This rising expenditure is substantially dedicated to modernizing military capabilities, and I think GD stands to benefit from this trend. Its portfolio which includes Virginia-Class submarines, Gulfstream tanks, Abrams tanks and Stryker vehicles aligns directly with the modernization need.

GD’s recent $12.4 billion contract award for its Virginia-Class submarine underscores its pivotal role in the US naval modernization, which is one of the highest spenders on military in the world. The contract combines with a backlog of orders across the defense segment, providing revenue visibility for years to come.

As opposed to cyclical industries, defense spending tends to remain resilient during economic downturns as national security stands to be a priority. This company’s exposure to long-term government contracts improves cash flow stability, making this company a defensive investment in an uncertain economic climate. Given the increasing recession odds, I think it’s time to consider this stock.

Dividend Aristocrat With Shareholder Appeal?

If you are an investor focusing on income, GD offers a compelling story. The company has a 30-year track record of consecutive dividend growth, compared to the sector median of 2.5 years and 33 years of consecutive dividend payments.

Its 10-year dividend CAGR of 8.49% is more than 18% higher than the sector median of 7.18%. Above all, its yield is significantly higher than the sector median, making it more attractive than its peers.

Most importantly, with a dividend payout ratio of 46.49% TTM, its dividend policy is highly sustainable and has ample room for growth. I am delighted by the company's capital allocation. For instance, in Q1 2025, the company spend $383 million on dividend payments, $600 million in share repurchase and $142 million in capital expenditure. This disciplined capital allocation demonstrates a commitment to balancing growth and shareholder returns.

Tenacity Amid Policy Uncertainty

The economic climate in 2025 is characterized by tariff concerns, inflation and potential slowdowns as shown by US economic contraction in Q1 2025.

Although these factors have led to volatility in the stock market, GD’s defensive nature has offered a buffer. Its reliance on government contracts insulates it from consumer driven economic cycles, and its global customer base mitigates risks associated with US-specific policy changes.

Its tenacity is shown by the 3.28% stock gains YTD, compared to S&P’s 3.93% loss. I think this further demonstrates investors’ confidence in the company’s fundamentals.

Valuation

From a comparative PE valuation approach, GD is undervalued. With a trailing GAAP PE of 18.95 which is below the sector median of 22.67 and a forward PE ratio of 18.31 compared to the sector median of 20.32, it follows that this stock is undervalued. This is not justifiable when I look at its diluted EPS YoY growth of 17.51% which is above the sector median of 5.90% and its forward diluted EPS growth rate of 11.56% compared to the sector median of 8.09%.

In my opinion, these figures point out that GD should even trade at a premium. However, I estimate my price target using the projected EPS for 2025 ($14.92), and the sector median average forward PE of 20.32. I am adopting this PE because I expect the market to at least take this undervalued stock at par with its peers given its strong fundamentals highlighted in this article. These assumptions land me to a price estimate of about $303.17, translating to an 11.4% upside potential.

Risks

My bullish take isn’t without risks; some of the risks to my thesis are:

  • Government spending changes: In case government expenditure on defense is cut, this could result in lower demand, potentially hurting topline growth.
  • Geopolitical uncertainty: Although tensions drive demand, de-escalation could reduce the need for defense spending.

Conclusion

General Dynamics is a rare blend of defensive stability, attractive dividend, and a positive value gap. While there are risks worth watching, this company remains a buy in 2025 and beyond given the tough economic outlook and the company’s resilience in such times.

data.title: Tariff Uncertainty Clouds Long-Term Outlook For Tech Companies
data.description:
Tech companies are exploring short-term strategies to navigate global tariff uncertainty while holding off on long-term decisions about manufacturing and expansion.

It has been more than a month since the Trump administration announced a sweeping range of tariffs on imports from scores of countries, but executives and industry experts remain unclear on what duties will be in effect in the months and quarters to come. US President Donald Trump has imposed 145% tariffs on imports from China, while most other countries are subject to 10% tariffs amid a 90-day pause announced April 9. China has retaliated by raising tariffs on US goods to 125%, though Washington and Beijing have some exemptions in place for semiconductors. US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said the exemption is temporary, with chips and other electronics set to be covered under new sector-specific tariffs that will be announced in the near future.

The lack of clarity is complicating the outlook for tech companies, with multiple executives citing a lack of visibility beyond the second quarter. Longer term, expectations are low for the potential of major gains for US manufacturing to be realized in the next four years.

"There's a reluctance to move ahead, even with well-laid-out plans, until the whole trade framework [becomes] guidelines," said Stavros Gadinis, a professor and faculty director at the University of California's Berkeley Center for Law and Business. "This is an extreme reversal of times for the tech industry, and something that the industry was definitely not ready to deal with. This element of surprise is a big problem for companies."

Tariff troubles

Among the companies to feel the biggest impact from tariffs is Apple Inc. (AAPL), which has a well-established global supply chain. Although the company has taken steps to diversify away from mainland China, a significant portion of its manufacturing occurs in markets including India, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan and Vietnam.

To minimize the impact of the US tariffs on Chinese imports, Apple CEO Tim Cook told analysts this month that the majority of iPhones sold in the US in the June quarter would be built in India. Meanwhile, almost all iPads, Macs, Apple Watches and AirPods products sold in the US will have Vietnam as their country of origin. "China would continue to be the country of origin for the vast majority of total product sales outside the US," Cook said on the company's May 1 earnings call.

Although iPhones and other consumer electronics are exempt from the 145% tariffs placed on Chinese imports, they are subject to the 20% rate that Trump has tied to the international drug trade, particularly fentanyl.

"We estimate the impact, assuming that the current global tariff rates, policies and applications don't change for the balance of the quarter, to be $900 million to our costs," Cook said. "I wouldn't want to predict the mix of production in the future ... but I wanted to give you clarity for the June quarter of where the country of origins are so you can use that for your modeling."

Consumer sentiment from 451 Research's 2025 Macroeconomic Outlook, Consumer Spending, Sentiment & Expectations survey revealed that 60% of respondents expected consumer electronics to see price increases due to tariff policies on US trade partners.

A strategy businesses could deploy to reduce their exposure to added tariff-related costs is to bifurcate their hardware from embedded software, said Lynlee Brown, a partner in Ernst & Young's Global Trade practice.

Cook and NVIDIA Corp. CEO Jensen Huang have urged the Trump Administration to exempt their products from 145% tariffs imposed on Chinese imports. China dropped its tariffs on US microchips to zero, while maintaining its 125% retaliatory tariff on all other US imports.

Staying agile

Beyond tariffs, chipmakers are also navigating US policies that limit the sale of AI chips to China. In April, NVIDIA (NVDA) said it would record a write-down of up to $5.5 billion in its fiscal first quarter in the face of export restrictions on its H20 chips for the Chinese market.

Huang has pressed for the US to curtail those regulations.

"China is a very large market. It's probably going to be a $50 billion AI market in two or three years," Huang told CNBC on May 6. "It would be a tremendous loss not to be able to address it as an American company."

Also in April, NVIDIA announced plans to commission more than a million square feet of manufacturing space to build and test NVIDIA Blackwell chips in Arizona and AI supercomputers in Texas.

On CNBC, Huang stressed the importance of staying "agile" as a company. "Whatever the policies are of the government, whatever's in the best interest of our country, we'll support," he said.

Agility, though, is a luxury that is easier for some companies to afford than others. Larger, high-tech companies with more margin room can absorb impacts better than smaller manufacturers, said Ross Meyercord, CEO of Propel Software.

"One of the meta lessons to be learned here between COVID-19 and tariffs and trade wars is that resiliency and optionality are critical," Meyercord said. "We, of course, have to be cost-effective through this, but we need to find a way to blend optionality and alternate sources, alternate approaches as a way to mitigate risk."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Xometry: Tariffs May Not Be As Bad As Initially Feared (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Xometry's (NASDAQ:XMTR) Q1 results were strong, and the company gave solid forward guidance, particularly given the uncertain macro environment. The full impact of tariffs likely won't be known for some time, though, and the fact that Xometry recently conducted layoffs suggests that Xometry is feeling at least some pressure. Even though Xometry has relatively low direct exposure to tariffs, its business could still suffer if overall manufacturing activity declines.

I previously suggested that the fundamentals of Xometry’s business remained resilient despite increased macro uncertainty. The imposition of unexpectedly high tariffs has created additional headwinds since then, though, and as a result, manufacturing sentiment in the US has plummeted. Looking beyond this, there could be a reshoring tailwind if tariffs remain in place. Regardless, I am willing to look through any near term headwinds, as the custom manufacturing market is a large opportunity and Xometry only has a tiny share.

Market Conditions

Xometry's first quarter commentary and full-year guidance suggest that tariff headwinds are fairly modest at the moment. This isn't necessarily surprising given that Xometry has 18 localized marketplaces across the US, Europe and Asia and the vast majority of demand in the US was already fulfilled domestically.

While Xometry has seen an incremental shift towards domestic sourcing, low-cost offshore manufacturing is expected to remain important for some customers, and Xometry believes it can help meet this demand with its global network.

The full impact of tariffs likely won't be known for some time, but survey data already suggests a sharp decline in manufacturing activity in the US could be imminent. This represents a downside risk to Xometry's stock, given that it has more than fully recovered from the post "liberation day" drop.

Xometry Business Updates

The growth of Xometry's core business is being supported by the continued expansion of instant quoting capabilities. For example, Instant Quoting for Injection Molding was recently introduced in the EU, UK and Turkey. The company also plans on further adding to its Instant Quoting capabilities in 2025.

Xometry also continues to improve its Teamspace software, which is an important driver of adoption amongst enterprise customers. Teamspace is targeted at procurement teams managing assemblies and products, and appears to be broadly adopted amongst Xometry's larger customers. On the other side of the market, Xometry also continues to improve Workcenter, its cloud-based manufacturing execution system.

While Xometry's Marketplace continues to thrive, its Supplier Services business is languishing. Xometry is testing a new search experience on Thomasnet, which appears to be semantic search leveraging LLMs. The company also plans on introducing a new ad server, which will increase the inventory of advertising that can be sold on Thomasnet. Xometry appears to be facing a demand problem at the moment though, and it is not clear that these changes will help to address this.

Financial Analysis

Xometry generated 151 million USD revenue in Q1, an increase of 23% YoY. While this growth is strong, it came against a relatively easy comparable period in 2024. Marketplace revenue increased 27% to 136 million USD, driven by enterprise adoption, while Supplier Services revenue was down 6% YoY. Supplier Services was up sequentially though on the back of supplier financing. US marketplace growth reached nearly 30%, compared to 20% international growth. While international growth has decelerated, Xometry is still targeting 30-40% marketplace revenue contribution from international markets, compared to 18% in 2024.

Xometry expects 155-157 million USD revenue in Q2, an increase of 17-18% YoY. Marketplace revenue is expected to increase 20-22%, while Supplier Services revenue is expected to decline 5-7%. On the back of strong Q1 growth, Xometry also raised full-year guidance, and now anticipates 20-22% Marketplace growth, with Supplier Services revenue declining 5%.

The number of active buyers increased 22% YoY in the first quarter, reaching 71,454. Revenue per active buyer was also up 4% YoY, driven by enterprise adoption in the US. The number of accounts spending over 50,000 USD over the past 12 months increased 12% YoY. This is a positive given the weakness observed amongst this cohort in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Xometry's gross margin was 37.3% in the first quarter, with the sequential decline driven by a combination of mix shift and lower Marketplace gross margin. The dip in marketplace margin was expected due to investments in international markets. Marketplace gross margin is expected to rebound towards 34% in Q2.

While Xometry's gross margin dipped in the first quarter, revenue outperformance led to an improved adjusted EBITDA margin. Non-GAAP operating expenses increased 2% YoY to 56.4 million USD, driven by a reduction in sales and marketing expenses. As a result, Xometry's adjusted EBITDA was 100,000 USD in the quarter. This excludes 1.5 million USD of restructuring costs related to cost-cutting efforts that included a 5% workforce reduction.

Conclusion

Xometry's first quarter results and forward guidance were solid, helping to allay some of the concerns around potential tariff headwinds. The fallout won't really be known for some time, though, meaning it is still possible that Xometry's business slows in coming quarters. I don't believe that this is particularly concerning, though, due to the long-term potential of the business.

Xometry is targeting a large and fragmented market that it is still predominantly offline. It also continues to gain market share, which is important given that the competitive positioning of its marketplace should improve with scale.

Xometry is also still reasonably valued, despite its share price moving significantly higher over the past year. Xometry has the potential to generate solid double-digit revenue growth long-term and is targeting a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin target. As a result, I can see earnings growth driving Xometry's PE ratio into the single digits over the next 5-10 years, which should lead to strong returns.

data.title: DocuSign: Still The Most Dominant Digital Signature Software Company
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In this coverage, we analyzed DocuSign, Inc. (NASDAQ:DOCU), one of the top software providers in the global digital signature market, which has had a stellar 5-year revenue CAGR of 25.04% but now has a 3-year forward analysts’ consensus of only 7.4%. We investigate the sharp slowdown in the company’s revenue growth over the past two years and assess whether a recovery is likely. We start by analyzing whether the slowdown is tied to broader weakness in the global eSignature market. We then assess whether the company has lost competitiveness by comparing its flagship software with products from the leading players by market share. We also study the company’s business metrics, focusing on customer growth and average selling price trends, to understand how much they have contributed to the slowdown. Based on these findings, we forecast the company’s revenue growth prospects.

Macro Environment Weakness

In the first section we examined whether a factor for the company’s growth slowdown, which as seen in the table above, could be the result of the global eSignature market weakness given that the company claimed in its previous earnings transcript regarding “a tough macro environment” and that “companies are scrutinizing investments” which “leads to smaller expansion opportunities”. To verify management’s claim, we examine the bigger environment by compiling the global internet penetration rate, technology adoption rate in smartphones and PCs, and world globalization rate.

The chart above from KOF shows the globalization index for the world and the US, indicating a plateauing trend over the past decade since the 2010s, which, we believe, could affect demand growth for digital contract management and electronic signatures tied to international transactions.

We believe that the internet penetration rate acts as a major driver for the company, given that as internet access becomes more widespread, more businesses and individuals can engage in remote transactions, using services like DocuSign for secure digital signatures on contracts and official documents, which are often shared and signed via email. Based on the above, we compiled the global internet penetration percentage by dividing the global internet users by the world population. In 2024, the growth of the global internet penetration rate significantly slowed from 7.3% in 2022 to a flattish 1% in 2024, primarily due to a sharp slowdown in internet user growth from 8.14% in 2022 to 1.85% in 2024. We believe that the slowdown in internet penetration could be due to a market growth slowdown across developed countries, where a large portion of the national population already has access to the internet. According to DataReportal, the US internet penetration rate as of 2024 has already reached 97.1%, while other developed countries such as the UK and Germany also show a high internet penetration rate at 97.8% and 93.3% respectively.

We believe PC penetration is another key driver, given that increased PC penetration would allow more users in digital signature, such as in professional settings, to use DocuSign's platform in facilitating secure signing and management of business documents and contracts that are typically stored and conducted on PCs. Based on the above, there has been a growth slowdown in PC penetration over the years and amid that, PC market shipments have been weak with an average decline of -8.7% in the past 3 years. We believe this is attributable to market saturation of traditional PCs across developed countries, where a large portion of the national population already owns a PC, leading to fewer first-time buyers and slower growth. According to IBIS World, 95.7% of US households have at least one computer in 2023, while other developed regions such as Canada had a PC penetration of 83.9% in 2020 according to ITU Datahub.

We believe the smartphone penetration rate is also a key driver, as individuals can use smartphones to execute agreements and observed that the growth of the global smartphone penetration rate has also been consistently slowing down to a flattish growth averaging 3.6% over the past 3 years. We overall believe the company’s revenue slowdown may be partly explained by the maturing growth of the internet and the penetration rates. Based on our forecasts, we project internet, smartphone, and PC penetration growth rates to remain flattish due to these fundamental factors.

Competitiveness Software Features but at a Premium Pricing

We move on to assess whether the company’s revenue slowdown is driven by a loss of competitiveness in the global digital eSignature software market.

We compiled market share data from Datanyze and find that DocuSign’s share declined by 8.8% over the year, while competitors such as Xink, Adobe Sign (ADBE), Nitro, and SignNow gained between 0.6% and 3.5% share, highlighting rising competition for DocuSign from smaller companies, but it still leads with a solid market share of 68.2%, still a strong market position. To see how DocuSign software stacks against competitors, we compiled data including pricing, features and calculated a popularity score based on Gartner reviews.

From our comparison table above, DocuSign stands out in terms of popularity, as it has the highest score among competitors. We believe this reflects its first-mover advantage, with the company founded earlier than its competitors in 2003, compared to Xink in 2009 and Adobe, which only entered the market with EchoSign in 2011. We find that Adobe has a slight edge over DocuSign in terms of software feature count, though DocuSign is well ahead of smaller companies with a gap of 30 features. In terms of overall ranking, despite DocuSign’s strengths, our analysis shows Adobe comes out slightly ahead, as Adobe’s software is priced much lower than DocuSign’s, which has a premium subscription that may negatively affect demand from price-sensitive customers.

Considering pricing, features, and popularity rankings, we derive a total competitiveness score of 1.08x for the company and expect it to maintain a strong position in the digital signature software market, but we note rising competition from smaller players and premium pricing may pose challenges, still we believe its value offering is supported by feature-rich software. Management gave an insight into these developments in the latest earnings, stating that its product roadmap for FY2026 includes “Agreement AI, innovative new workflows, an expanded ecosystem, and powerful new capabilities for enterprise customers”. To assess whether it can continue enhancing its value proposition through feature development and R&D, we examine its financials in the next section.

Growth Driven by Expanding Customer Base

To further analyze the company’s growth performance, we examine its business metrics, such as customer and ASP growth, and compile the data in the table below for comparison with its overall growth performance.

Based on the above, we compiled the company’s customer base breakdown into enterprise and commercial customers and derived its non-enterprise customer count (individual users). We estimated ASP for enterprise and non-enterprise customers using a pricing ratio of 1 to 4.33 between business and individual plans, then calculated the respective pricing using the Excel Solver function. We observe that enterprise and commercial customer growth slowed more sharply, from 24.1% in FY2023 to 14.7% in FY2024 and further to 7.4% in FY2025. In comparison, non-enterprise customer growth also slowed but at a more moderate pace, from 17.1% in FY2023 to 15.5% in FY2024 and 14.5% in FY2025. Since FY2020, the revenue contribution from enterprise customers increased from 37.1% to 43.9% in FY2025, while the share from non-enterprise customers declined from 62.9% to 56.1%.

We observe that despite slower growth rates, the company has consistently added around 200,000 customers per year over the past three years. We believe that as DocuSign scales, sustaining high growth becomes increasingly difficult due to its already large market penetration, making new growth opportunities harder to capture. The percentage growth in customer base has declined even though absolute gains remain significant, which we believe reflects the maturity of its core markets. We also believe the ongoing slowdown in customer and ASP growth is closely linked to broader macro trends, as discussed earlier, with internet, PC, and smartphone penetration plateauing across major developed regions such as the US, UK, Germany, and Canada.

As seen in the chart, the company has paid down its debt over the past 3 years, improving its cash-to-debt ratio to 7.7x. We believe this strong financial position provides opportunities for future acquisitions, which could support further feature development and enhance competitiveness in the global digital signature market. The company has made a series of acquisitions in the past, such as SpringCM, Seal Software, and Clause, mainly to enhance its capabilities in document management, AI, and workflow automation, and we believe its recent acquisition in 2024 of Lexion could strengthen its feature set and boost its competitiveness. From its latest earnings, management gave a hint by highlighting the “integrated additional powerful Agreement AI capabilities” from Lexion, which give benefits as “IAM customers have reduced their contracting cycles by up to 75%”.

Based on the above, we project the company’s total customer base to increase by a consistent 190,000 per year through 2028, based on its past 7-year average. For ASP, which declined in FY2025, we conservatively forecast it using the past 3-year average of -2.91%, resulting in a projected 3-year forward average revenue growth of 6.9%. Our FY2026 forecast is also in line with management’s guidance of 5% YoY growth from its latest transcript. We also believe it is worth noting the company’s improved financial position, which could provide future additional growth opportunities for M&A and R&D investments to enhance its digital signature software features and overall value to customers.

Risk: Low Barriers to Entry of Software Market

According to the Canvas Business Model, the global eSignature market has “low barriers to entry due to technological advancements and the proliferation of cloud computing,” and the initial investment to launch a digital signature business can be relatively modest, often estimated at up to $50,000 for a basic software product. We believe this indicates a notable threat of new entrants, which could lead to increased competition and potential price pressure, especially given DocuSign’s relatively expensive pricing as discussed in the second point.

Verdict

We believe DocuSign’s recent revenue slowdown compared to its past may be due to the plateauing of key adoption drivers, including internet, PC, and smartphone penetration, which we expect to remain flat over the next few years. But despite this, we still believe DocuSign maintains a solid competitive position, supported by its strong feature set, though we note increasing pressure from rising competition and its premium pricing. We project an average revenue growth of 6.9% over the next three years, which, we believe, is in line with management’s FY2026 guidance of 5% growth and consistent with the 3-year forward analysts’ consensus. We also believe DocuSign’s strengthened financial position, with the paired debt and increased cash, could enable it to pursue future acquisitions and invest in R&D, such as the 2024 acquisition of Lexion to enhance its AI capabilities. We thus believe DocuSign could stay competitive in the digital signature market.

For our valuation of the company, we used a DCF model, as it has positive FCF margins (5-year average of 22.8%). While revenue growth has slowed, we expect stable revenue growth averaging 5.7% annually over the next five years. We also note that profitability could drive further upside, with operating margins improving from -49% in 2015 to 7.8% in 2024, and we project this to rise to 20.45% by 2029 as the company scales. Using a discount rate of 10.9% (the company’s WACC) and a terminal value based on the top software industry companies' 5-year average EV/EBITDA of 27.62x, our model indicates an upside of 11.49%. With the stock down 9.2% YTD, we rate the company as a Buy.

data.title: RPM International: Dividends, Modest Growth, And A Cautious Outlook
data.description:
Company Description

RPM International (NYSE:RPM) produces chemicals for a variety of end markets and has operations worldwide. The company has four reportable segments, Construction Products, Performance Coatings, Specialty Products, and Consumers Group. RPM employs more than 17,000 people worldwide, and its current market cap is around $14B. The stock has slightly underperformed the broad market over the past decade, returning almost 180%, or about 10.8% annually.

Quality Financial Metrics

I believe high-quality companies should exhibit three key quantitative characteristics: a growing revenue stream, a stable or improving gross profit margin, and a healthy return on invested capital. Let's examine these metrics for RPM to assess its performance.

Over the past decade, the revenue per share metric has grown at an average rate of 5.5% annually. Growth was steady between FY15 and FY20, with more robust increases in recent years. In fact, from FY21 through FY23, the revenue per share grew at nearly 10% per year. However, FY24 saw a slight decline, so it's worth monitoring FY25 results to determine if growth will resume.

The gross profit margin exceeded 40% for FY15 through FY17 but remained below this level from FY18 until FY23. In FY24, the margin eclipsed 41%, suggesting a potential rebound. Ideally, the GPM will strengthen and stabilize above 40% for the foreseeable future.

The return on invested capital has been relatively stable, typically around 10%. Recently, this metric has shown a slight upward trend, reaching a chart high in FY24 at more than 12.5%. Ideally, the ROIC can continue to grow and eventually reach my target threshold of at least 20%.

Overall, these metrics are solid, with the revenue per share showing consistent growth, the return on invested capital trending higher, and the gross profit margin showing signs of recovery. However, all three metrics should be monitored going forward to see if the recent improvements can be sustained.

Dividend Data

Recently, RPM achieved Dividend King status, raising its dividend for more than 50 consecutive years. The company currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.51, for a total annual dividend of $2.04, and a resulting yield of about 1.88%. The 10-year dividend growth rate is pretty good at 6.5%, especially for a company with such a lengthy dividend history. After spiking in FY17, the payout ratio has been on a downward trend, including dropping below 40% in FY21 and FY24. Assuming this low payout ratio continues, RPM should be able to continue rewarding its shareholders with at least mid-single-digit dividend increases moving forward.

Q3 2025 Earnings

About a month ago, RPM International announced their 2025 Q3 earnings with a double-miss, GAAP EPS came up $0.05 short of estimates at $0.40 a share on revenue of $1.48B which also missed expectations by about $30M. Additionally, normalized EPS was $0.35 missing the consensus by $0.15.

Looking at the segments, the Construction Products Group saw sales decline 4.5% from $495M in 2024 to $473M in 2025. Unfavorable weather conditions led to reduced construction and restoration activity in key markets. Moving on to the Performance Coatings Group, sales for Q3 were $340M, a decrease of less than 1% compared to 2024. Although there was a minor decline, this sales number was solid, just up against strong growth from the prior year. The Specialty Products Group struggled the most with a more double-digit decline in revenue, $176M to $158M, as a result of lower demand in specialty OEM end markets along with lower remediation activity, this decrease was partially offset by a slight increase in food coatings. And lastly, (the) Consumer Group had sales slip less than 1% YoY to $503M, driven by market share gains as well as new products, but was partially offset by weak foreign currency translation.

Management also provided guidance for Q4 2025, where they expect overall sales to be flat year-over-year with some segments performing better than others. The stock swung quite a bit around earnings, dropping more than 9% the day prior to earnings, while on April 8th, the day of earnings, it rose almost 9%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The final consideration is to determine if RPM is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

At its current price, my custom valuation model suggests RPM is trading for a moderate discount to its fair value, about 12%. The stock price doesn't typically go through any drastic changes, although the FCF does. There was a noticeable FCF jump in 2024 to more than $7 per share; however, looking at the last twelve months, this number has declined to less than $4.50 per share. The stock has struggled thus far in 2025, down a little more than 10%, leading to its possible current undervaluation.

The long-term expected rate of return for RPM is nearly 12% with the components of that estimate as follows: a dividend yield of 1.88%, a return to fair value factor of 2.47%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 7.75%.

In conclusion, RPM has done well over the past decade, but has slightly underperformed the broad market. The financial metrics are decent and all appear to be trending in the right direction; however, the next earnings report should be telling. RPM's dividend streak is very impressive, reaching more than 50 years recently, couple that with a low payout ratio and dividend increases should be easily supported. The valuation is appealing, but the fact that each segment of the company saw sales decline in the most recent earnings report gives me pause. Ultimately, I rate this stock a HOLD until the next earnings report hopefully provides a more clear direction.

data.title: The Fed Is In A 'Good Place' - But For How Long?
data.description:
Fed officials remain patient, likely awaiting hard evidence of a weaker U.S. labor market before considering rate cuts.

The Federal Reserve left interest rates on hold, as widely expected. The main takeaway from the statement and press conference was uncertainty: Fed Chair Jerome Powell repeatedly emphasized the unclear economic outlook as the central bank’s rate-setting committee met for the first time since the 2 April tariff announcements.

The Fed statement directly acknowledged that these trade policy changes have increased risks to both sides of its dual mandate: Tariffs are likely to weaken growth and spill over to the labor market, even as they raise prices.

If these trends become clearer in data releases in the coming months (which we expect, though the latest inflation and employment reports were benign), the Fed will likely be in a challenging spot.

We believe the Fed will hold off on rate cuts until hard evidence of a slowdown appears in labor market reports – potentially not until late summer or fall. Once that evidence arrives, however, we expect the Fed will cut rates quickly to support the economy.

In a good place in May…

As Powell indicated at the press conference, the Fed seems to be in a good place and can be patient. The April U.S. employment report suggests the labor market is doing well, and inflation is still somewhat above target, so the Fed has some luxury to wait for more information before making any decisions that take monetary policy out of restrictive territory.

Powell also stated that, given elevated inflation, the Fed wouldn’t cut rates preemptively in an effort to get ahead of higher recession risks. Markets seemed undisturbed following the relatively quiet May Fed meeting, with U.S. Treasury yields ending the day near their pre-announcement levels.

… but ‘a complicated and challenging’ outlook

While there were few signs of disruption in the most recent inflation and employment data, Powell emphasized the uncertain and challenging outlook for Fed officials. Initial conditions of a relatively solid economy and above-target inflation, plus the economic implications of tariffs, put the central bank in a tricky spot.

With tariffs, the near-term impacts tend to be higher inflation but lower real economic activity, including employment. Indeed, the Fed revised its May statement relative to the prior release in March to acknowledge that risks of this conflicting outcome have increased.

Thus, the central bank must balance the risks and potential benefits of policy decisions: lowering rates to support growth and employment (risking higher inflation), or keeping rates elevated to help restrain inflation and anchor inflation expectations (risking higher unemployment). Waiting for clarity appears to be the central bank’s best strategy for now, as Powell made clear at this meeting.

In order to cut rates, we think the Fed will need to be confident that inflation expectations are well-anchored and recession risks are rising conclusively. This assurance may not come until the data show definitive evidence that the labor market is decelerating or contracting. In response to questions about cutting rates amid rising recession risks, Powell acknowledged that “it's not a situation where we can be preemptive.”

Tariffs should be expected to produce only a fleeting burst of inflation (euphemistically called a price level adjustment). But Fed officials are acutely aware of the risks that come with both the scale and timing of this episode.

As companies pass some of the tariff costs on to consumers, core U.S. inflation could well breach 4%. With memories of the pandemic’s inflationary surge still fresh, the danger is that increased inflation expectations could stoke nominal wage growth. That would make it harder for the Fed to bring inflation back to target without a firmer hand on policy.

Worryingly, inflation expectations are rising again. Surveys show that households, businesses, and forecasters are all bracing for higher inflation, with one-year-ahead expectations now back at pandemic-era highs – well before any broad-based evidence of firms raising prices. Longer-term measures, such as the University of Michigan’s five-year, five-year-ahead gauge, have climbed to levels unseen in a quarter-century.

We believe this means that Fed officials are likely to proceed with caution, waiting for clear evidence that inflation expectations remain anchored and labor markets are softening before making their next move.

Tariffs and monetary policy: lessons from history

A World Bank study of 16 advanced economies from 1960 to 2019 finds that, on average, central banks raised policy rates by 13 basis points (bps) for every percentage point increase in effective tariff rates. The response, however, was not symmetric across economic environments.

In periods when inflation was running hot – specifically, when the three-year average exceeded 4% – central banks were far more hawkish, lifting short-term rates by as much as 40 bps for each percentage point increase in tariffs. In cooler, low-inflation environments, policy rates barely budged. Notably, in neither scenario was cutting rates the prevailing response.

Currently, the data indicate the U.S. is likely in the former (hotter) camp: As of March, the three-year average for headline inflation stands at 4.6%. History suggests this would tend to keep the Fed holding steady. Both markets and policymakers see little chance of rate hikes.

Bottom line

Given the next Fed rate cut will likely require evidence of a weakening labor market, exactly when it might start to contract is crucial. Trade policy, shifting immigration rules, federal government layoffs, and a general air of uncertainty are all expected to weigh on the labor market at different points this year.

For now, we believe the risk is that any real deterioration will not show up until summer – which means (given lags in the data) that hard evidence of a labor market slowdown may not be in hand until the Fed’s late July or – more likely – mid-September meeting.

Even if trade tensions de-escalate and tariffs moderate, the U.S. will still face import duties higher than anything seen in recent history. The near-term consequences are clear: higher inflation, lower real incomes, and contracting investment. The longer-term effects are less clear, but they could include greater investment in U.S. industries – time will tell.

For now, these economic challenges put the Fed in a tough spot. The evidence needed to prompt a rate cut likely won’t appear until later this summer or early fall. Once it does, we expect the Fed to cut rates fairly quickly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: The Stock Everyone Ignored Until 2022
data.description:
Sprouts Farmers Market (NASDAQ:SFM) shares have risen sharply in recent years as the company continues to grow strongly. Sprouts is a well-known name in Phoenix, Arizona, where it started in 2002 and has since grown into a national chain.

In 2013, the company went public, but the share price showed little growth in the first few years. At the time, Sprouts mainly tried to achieve sales growth with aggressive discount promotions, such as sending out coupons for sharp prices on fresh products. This put pressure on profit margins and affected price developments.

In 2019, former Walmart executive vice president Jack Sinclair took over the CEO position to restore profitability. Sinclair abolished loss-making promotions and even stopped paper advertising altogether in favor of margin improvement. This change of strategy initially led to lower sales growth but laid the foundation for significantly better results. Sprouts has managed to structurally increase profit margins (the operating margin has been above 7% since 2020) while improving revenue figures. The stock has risen sharply in recent years to record highs of $170. Investors clearly have confidence in CEO Jack Sinclair and his new strategy.

In my article, I discuss this transformation and what it means for the future. I also look at the prospects and see whether the stock is worth buying.

Sprouts’ Unique Niche And Growth Catalysts

The grocery industry is huge, with annual sales in the U.S. approaching $1 trillion. Within that market, consumer preferences are changing as more consumers value healthy foods and organic produce. Also, the strong demand for weight-loss products like Ozempic shows that consumers want to keep their bodies healthy. Sprouts Farmers Market has capitalized on this trend and is benefiting from this long-term trend. More than 70 percent of its products are organic, gluten-free, non-GMO or health-focused.

What makes Sprouts strong is that it operates in a niche market, which means it does not have to compete directly on price with supermarket giants. But for now, Sprouts is selling its products below Whole Foods' retail prices, making the company attractive to budget-conscious consumers.

Sprouts has several growth catalysts for further growth. The demand for natural and organic food is growing strongly, and Sprouts also sees this in e-commerce sales. Sales have increased strongly (28% revenue growth) thanks to Sprouts' investment in delivery partnerships, e-commerce turnover now accounts for 15% of total revenue. In addition, Sprouts is launching its first loyalty program in the second half of this year after several successful pilots. Such a loyalty program enables personalized promotions, which is expected to increase revenue per customer.

The opening of new stores will drive further growth. At least 35 new stores will open this year, an 8% increase over the current store count. Sprouts will expand in local markets like the Southwest, as well as new markets like the Midwest and Northeast. This will allow the company to gain market share in regions where consumers currently shop at Whole Foods.

Additionally, Sprouts will begin supplying stores from its new distribution center in the third quarter of 2025 and will add additional capacity in Northern California next year. These supply chain solutions should provide long-term cost benefits.

Finally, Sprouts’ private label is a growth engine, as 24% of sales come from Sprouts’ private label products. This is beneficial, as these products are sold at higher margins and strengthen Sprouts’ private label.

Healthy Financials

Sprouts is growing strongly, with revenue up 19% YoY to $2.2 billion in the first quarter of this year. This was partly due to the opening of new stores, but also due to the increase in comparable sales. Existing Sprouts stores generated 11.7% more sales than the same period last year, demonstrating the growing popularity of the formula, both through increased customer numbers and higher revenue per customer.

Profitability has also improved. Gross margin was 39.6% in the first quarter of 2025 (up 129 basis points compared to Q1 2024). This was due to better inventory management and category management, which allowed Sprouts to purchase more efficiently. As a result, Sprouts reported net income of $180 million, which is 58% higher than the $114 million in the first quarter of 2024. Adjusted earnings per share rose a strong 62% YoY to $1.81, and its outlook for FY 2025 is also strong. Sprouts expects sales growth of 12% to 14% this year, with comparable store sales growth of 5.5% to 7.5%.

However, it is not certain whether Sprouts has included the risks of Trump's import tariffs in its outlook. The question is to what extent these tariffs could affect Sprouts. Sprouts sources some of its assortment, such as seasonal vegetables/fruits outside the North American season, tropical fruits, coffee, tea, spices, and the like, from abroad. Import tariffs on products from Mexico (supplier of avocados, vegetables) would make these items more expensive. If Sprouts passes on these tariffs, this could reduce sales volumes. Or it could be at the expense of Sprouts' margin. Both scenarios are unfavorable for profitability. And higher tariffs on steel and building materials could also increase the costs of new store openings. It is not yet certain whether Sprouts has included these costs in its forecast for this year.

Valuation

Sprouts rewards shareholders with a share buyback program instead of a dividend payment. This is unusual because industry peers such as Kroger and Albertsons both pay dividends. Investors have not had to worry in recent years because Sprouts has already delivered a nice return in recent years.

The valuation of the stock is a point of discussion because it is not cheap. Based on the forward P/E ratio, Sprouts is very highly valued compared to similar companies. Investors seem very enthusiastic about Sprouts' growth prospects and are paying a high premium compared to sector peers. This premium is due to a combination of higher revenue growth, higher margins, and the niche nature of Sprouts.

Final Thoughts

Sprouts Farmers Market has shown strong growth in recent years under the leadership of CEO Jack Sinclair. The company has developed into a profitable and growing player with a niche market in healthy food. The recent quarterly figures show this: Sprouts is benefiting from changing consumer preferences and has growth engines such as store expansion, e-commerce and a new loyalty program which are driving further growth. Against these strong growth expectations is a valuation that already prices in the growth. The share price has increased fivefold in recent years and Sprouts now trades at a significant premium compared to its peers (and also historically). There is therefore less room for error. And risks such as a trade war could throw a spanner in the works. Sprouts has attractive long-term prospects, but the valuation is high and there are risks lurking. New investors would therefore be wise to wait for a good buying opportunity before getting in. Sprouts has therefore been given a "Hold" rating.

data.title: SBC Medical: Scalable Profits Undermined By Governance Overhang, Still Worth A Look
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Earnings Quality Analyst as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

SBC Medical Group Holdings (NASDAQ:SBC) represents a highly undervalued opportunity that enjoys high return on capital invested backed by a scalable, asset-light model in Japan's thriving aesthetic services market. Although its underlying fundamentals are strong, based on expansion in its clinic network, rising inbound patient flows, and high efficiency in capital deployment, the market has factored in bearish projections because of governance risks, related-party structures, and Japan-specific regulation overhang. Our residual earnings approach tells us that even after factoring in a conservative 15% to 20% cost of equity to factor in these risks, SBC is discounting a steep and unlikely decline in economic profitability. For investors willing to tolerate governance secrecy, SBC offers a high margin of safety and asymmetric potential.

Catalysts

  • Clinic network expansion: The company added 50 new clinics in FY24, and continue to expand clinics underservice, enhancing future service revenue base.
  • Operating leverage: Incremental clinic additions require limited added SG&A spend, and this will improve margins of operation.
  • Brand moat: SBC benefits from scale in digital marketing and patient acquisition. Particularly strong traction on Chinese social media platforms such as Xiaohongshu (“Red Note”). This has driven a surge of customers from China seeking cosmetic procedures at SBC’s clinics in Japan.
  • Undervalued float potential: Very high ROIC and free cash flow generation, as the business model requires little capex to operate. The majority of the costs are personnel expenses.

Business Overview

SBC Medical Group Holdings is a management company of a large network of franchise cosmetics clinics. Over 90% of these clinics are in Japan with the rest operates in the U.S. and Singapore. SBC provides back office operating activities to its network of partner clinics including marketing, HR functions, and loyalty reward points management, etc.

SBC right now is offering services to eight medical corporations (MCs), which own all the 251 clinics. These MCs are related parties to the SBC because the CEO's family members are in the management teams of those MCs, and the CEO himself was one until July 2023. SBC has an equity investment in six of the MCs as well, which can enable SBC to claim a portion of the residue in future. In reality, SBC owns and enjoys financial benefits from these clinics, but indirectly because of the restrictions imposed by the regulations.

How does SBC make money?

SBC 2024 10-k

SBC earns mostly five streams of revenue:

Franchising Revenue: These are basically brand licensing fees. These are fees that clinics pay for the use of the SBC brand name, trademarks, and systems. The revenue is earned over a period, as SBC provides ongoing brand and consulting support. It rose 45% in 2024 to $61 million from $42 million in 2023.

This was due largely to:

  1. Conversion of percentage royalty payments into fixed payments per clinic, and the expansion in the number of clinics.
  2. Patent and trademark licensing to the six major medical corporations starting in September 2023.
  3. Overall business expansion of the MCs. It represents 29.7% of SBC's total revenue in 2024.

Procurement services revenue: SBC is an intermediary purchasing items like medical equipment and advertising services for clinics. The revenue is earned when the products are shipped, and services are earned over time because the service is consumed. If SBC gets bulk purchase discounts, they remit over-collections to clinics. Procurement revenue increased by 3.1% to $54.8 million in 2024 compared to $53.2 million in 2023. Growth was mainly caused by increased demand for medical supplies by the increasing medical corporations (MCs), though this was somewhat muted by the fall of the Japanese yen. It constitutes 26.7% of SBC's total revenue in 2024.

Management Services Revenue: It is a collection of services SBC provides to franchise clinics (MCs). The bundle includes loyalty program management, labor support (now mostly on hold), functional support (e.g., HR, IT), and consulting services (SBC helps clinics operate their business, specifically in cosmetic dermatology). Management services revenue declined by 26.5% in 2024 to $53.1 million from $72.3 million in 2023. The cause of the decline was mainly:

  1. Termination of staff support services by Shobikai Sub in Q3, since the company will merge it with Lange Sub (which will render Shobikai Sub's business license invalid).
  2. Fall in revenue from loyalty program with less handling fee (5% to 4%) and increased free point redemptions.
  3. Depreciation of Japanese yen.

These falls were partially balanced by:

  1. New revenue with two more medical corporations added in September 2023.
  2. Expansion in business within existing MCs.
  3. Increased number of clinics. It represents 25.9% of SBC 2024 total revenue.

Rental Revenue: SBC rents out medical equipment or facilities to MCs. This segment includes two types of leases. Operating leases revenue is evenly distributed over time and Sales-type leases recognized revenue when lease terms are initially entered into. Recurring services revenue came close to doubling in 2024, at a 120% increase to $16.1 million from $7.3 million for 2023. This rise was mainly spurred by increased demand for the MCs' healthcare equipment, but it was to some extent eased by the reduced Japanese yen. It represents 7.9% of SBC 2024 total revenues.

Other Revenue: This is a mix of small miscellaneous items like Accommodation services, sale of medicines, brokerage fees, training, interest income, etc. These are recognized when SBC delivers the product or service. This segment earned $20.3 million in 2024, up by 9% from $18.6 million in 2023. This increase was mainly due to the growth of the subsidiary acquired in April 2023. It represents 9.9% of SBC's total revenue.

Overall, SBC revenue was $205.4 million in 2024, up 6.4% from $193.5 million in 2023. Perhaps the most important driver for SBC's future revenue growth is the number of clinics it has or has on its behalf. Since SBC earns a fixed fee per clinic, having more clinics directly increases its franchising revenue. The number of new patients coming to the clinics is also an important driver. More patients equate to higher activity in SBC's loyalty program, which translates directly to its management services revenue. With more patients earning and redeeming loyalty points, SBC captures more fees from managing these programs. Finally, the value of the Japanese yen against the U.S. dollar also matters. SBC gets paid in yen but reports in dollars. Thus, when yen is depreciating, the profits look smaller in USD even if the company is growing in Japan.

Growth drivers analysis

SBC 2024 Investor's Deck

SBC customers MC currently operates 251 Clinics in 2024, up 15.1% from 218 Clinics in 2023, and over 90% of Clinics are located in Japan. It is planning to expand its overseas clinics, especially in the US and Southeast Asia. For example, it had acquired Aesthetic Healthcare Holdings ("AHH") in November 2024, operating 4 aesthetic and beauty brands with 21 clinics in Singapore. Therefore, its future clinics under management number is expected to rise, which will benefit the franchising revenue growth, procurement revenue growth, and management services revenue growth. Clinics' inbound customer count has shown strong growth for SBC Medical Group.

In 2024, in-bound customers increased by 51%, from 7,229 in 2023 to 10,917. Chinese customers drove this increase and represented most of the number (8,009 in 2024 versus 4,590 in 2023). This will drive future management services revenue because in-bound customer growth directly drives loyalty program activity, clinic operations, and consulting support, all of which are included in SBC's management services revenue. A reasonable base case assumption is that SBC's total revenue will rise by approximately 10% year-over-year in the next 2 to 3 years, fueled by steady clinic growth, rising inbound patient volumes, and incremental international expansion.

A reasonable base case assumption is that SBC’s overall revenue will grow at approximately 10% year-over-year over the next 2 to 3 years, supported by steady clinic expansion, rising inbound patient volume, and gradual international growth.

Margins and capital efficiency

On the margin side, SBC had $85.4 million non-GAAP operating income in 2024 (excluding intangibles impairment, misappropriation loss one-time items) up 20.1% from $71.1 million operating income in 2023. Its operating margin rose from 36.7% in 2023 to 41.6% in 2024 as a result of SBC's cost-saving initiatives and Shobikai Sub's abandonment of clinic operation support services in Q3. Following this realignment, the respective staff were terminated, and a significant reduction in labor costs was realized. They are all one-off solutions to the margin, not to be repeated in the future as a pattern. It is logical to anticipate they will maintain their current operating margin of 36.7%.

I recalculated the firm's balance sheet to isolate assets that are actually operating and directly related to the core business activities. By stripping out operating-related liabilities from operating assets, I arrived at the net operating assets, which reflect the capital that shareholders have essentially put into the firm's core operations.

SBC's net operating asset turnover during 2024 remained very high at 4.31, though 0.4 lower than a year earlier. Operating asset turnover rose by 0.25, mainly due to the sale of subsidiaries that made the company more capital-effective. Operating liability leverage decreased significantly from 76.9% to 46.6% through enormous reductions in advances from customers and notes payable. These decreases were mainly due to loyalty point redemptions and the passing of non-recurring related-party payables. Since the gains in capital efficiency were primarily due to one-time items, it can be safely expected that SBC's future capital efficiency will be consistent at the current levels, with a gradual possible softening in the long run.

When we thought about how much after-tax operating profit, SBC's net operating assets generates (assuming 34.69% Japan Statutory Tax Rate). Its return on net operating assets (or ROIC excluding cash and equity investments) of 116.9% in 2024 and 112.6% in 2023 are staggering. This means that SBC is a very profitable company with the number of assets it put into the company. There are, however, risks to the fundamentals of the company.

Risk Analysis

The Japan aesthetic medical market has grown at a 9% CAGR (2020-2023), but the facilities have increased faster, at 12% CAGR. This means that supply is outpacing demand, putting a lot of pressure on prices and clinical profitability. Regulators in Japan are beginning to raise concerns about the rapid growth of aesthetic services. Urban doctor shortages and consumer grievances about high-pressure selling and medical error issues could result in greater regulation of the cosmetic trade. In addition, SBC has historically been a Japan-centric operator. Its internationalization to build overseas is promising, but is vulnerable to implementation. SBC's management also does not have deep experience running clinics abroad, especially in diverse regulatory and cultural environments like Southeast Asia and the U.S. Mistakes in adapting their model abroad can harm their future profitability or decelerate revenue growth.

On the side of control, CEO Yoshiyuki Aikawa is the largest shareholder of ~ 90% of outstanding common shares of the company and also serves as Chairman. Having one individual both as CEO and Chairman leads to excessive concentration of power, potentially stifling the board's independence and increasing the risk of self-dealing or entrenchment. Moreover, a significant majority of SBC's core clinic partners (MCs) are related parties with previous or current relationships with the CEO and his family.

This can be attributed to my higher risks of unequal pricing, financial engineering, and weak minority shareholder protection. Therefore, would-be investors will be subjected to high risks and thus should insist on a highly required return when investing in this company. The actual size of the capital surcharge you desire to impose on this company is left to the reader.

Valuation analysis

I recommend at least 15% required return when considering investment in SBC. The additional return over the minimum required return that SBC earned on shareholders' invested capital (equivalent to the net operating assets) grew 20.8% in 2024. SBC is currently trading at $3.1 a share with 103,611,251 basic shares outstanding. Its common equity on its balance sheet is $195,109,157 with no off-balance sheet debt or in-the money stock options outstanding. With the assumption of 15% required return, the current price would imply that the residual value would decrease by 20.66% infinitely in the future. Even with the assumption of 20% required return, the current price would imply that the extra value which the SBC will create would decrease by 16% infinitely in the future.

It essentially implies that the revenue of the company would reduce significantly and profit margins will be affected by an enormous degree in the future. Whether to invest in SBC is ultimately a question of your view of the company's future. If you think SBC will be able to sustain its profit and revenue levels despite industry headwinds, then the stock appears fairly undervalued and could be a good opportunity. But if you think structural issues in the aesthetics field will bring SBC's business down to zero or down materially, then it may be best to avoid it. Alternatively, if you object to SBC's opacity regarding its business structure, related-party transactions, or risk of governance, you might expect an asked-for return significantly in excess of 20% to offset the risk. I suspect at current prices, the stock is pricing in an unrealistic degradation of economic profitability.

Key Risks Summary

  • Regulatory risk: Japan’s Ministry of Health could impose stricter oversight on aesthetic medical services. This may affect SBC's ability to market for its servicing clinics and its franchise model.
  • Franchise dependency: SBC does not own clinics directly; reliance on third-party performance introduces operating variability.
  • Perception risk: Investors unfamiliar with Japan’s medical service corporation system may misinterpret revenue sustainability or confuse SBC with direct healthcare providers.
  • FX exposure: Yen depreciation could affect USD-reported earnings for international investors.

Conclusion

Currently, the market is expecting SBC's economic fundamentals to deteriorate significantly. With the increasing clinics under coverage and improving inbound customers from China, I don't see how that pessimistic story makes sense for the future of SBC. However, the SBC may have governance issue as the majority of SBC customers are related parties, CEO/Board Director dual identity, and majority of CEO insider holdings of common stock outstanding. Investors need to require a higher required return for SBC's common equity. For those who are willing to tolerate governance risk, SBC represents a deep value opportunity.

data.title: Fed's Interest Rate Decision: May 7, 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Federal Reserve concluded its third meeting of the year by keeping the federal funds rate (FFR) at 4.25-4.50%, as expected. This marks the third straight meeting the Fed has left rates unchanged.

Here is a statement from the meeting:

Although swings in net exports have affected the data, recent indicators suggest that economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace. The unemployment rate has stabilized at a low level in recent months, and labor market conditions remain solid. Inflation remains somewhat elevated.

The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further. The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate and judges that the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.

In support of its goals, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 4-1/4 to 4-1/2 percent. In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks. The Committee will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage‑backed securities. The Committee is strongly committed to supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.

In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

Background on the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The federal funds rate is the interest rate that banks charge each other to borrow money overnight. It is set by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), a committee within the Federal Reserve, which meets eight times a year. It is a primary tool used by the Federal Reserve to implement monetary policy and is a key driver of economic activity.

While it directly affects short-term borrowing between banks, the effects of the FFR can be felt across a variety of entities. For consumers, changes in the FFR influence mortgage rates, credit card interest, auto loans, and saving yields. For businesses, it affects borrowing costs and investment decisions. Additionally, financial markets also react to rate changes, with shifts in bond yields and equity performance.

The FOMC adjusts interest rates based on key economic indicators focusing on inflation, employment, economic growth, and income. The Fed has a dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment.

Historical Trends of the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The stagflation crisis of the late 1970s and early 1980s demanded drastic measures. Under the leadership of Paul Volcker, the Federal Reserve pushed the FFR to a historic high of 20.06% in January 1981. This aggressive tightening of monetary policy was instrumental in curbing runaway inflation, albeit at the cost of a significant economic slowdown.

In stark contrast, the FFR was driven to near-zero levels in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis and again during the economic turmoil of the 2020 pandemic. Specifically, the FFR reached a record low of approximately 0.04% in May 2020. These periods of ultra-low interest rates aimed to stimulate borrowing, investment, and economic recovery.

The federal funds rate has undergone significant fluctuations in the past two decades. Following the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed kept rates near zero until 2016. A gradual tightening cycle brought the rate to 2.25-2.50% by 2019, but the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic led to a return to near-zero rates in 2020.

In response to soaring inflation - the highest in four decades - the Fed aggressively raised rates from March 2022 to August 2023, reaching a peak of 5.25-5.50%, the highest level since early 2001. The central bank then shifted course in September 2024, implementing three consecutive rate cuts to bring the FFR to its current range of 4.25-4.50%.

Federal Funds Rate: What's Next?

The CME FedWatch Tool estimates the probability of future interest rate moves. The tool is updated in real-time in response to economic data releases, Fed statements, and market movements. The chart below shows the tool’s predictions through the end of 2026. (Note: The chart below is at the time of writing, and expectations may have shifted since publication.)

The market currently expects three rate cuts in 2025 at the July, September, and October meetings, with two additional cuts in 2026.

The Fed’s next meeting is scheduled for June 17-18th.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: First Watch Restaurant: Breakfast Slump Scrambles The Stock
data.description:
Like I said earlier, leaning into daytime traffic in Q1 was a misstep. Almost like shooting yourself in the foot.

Data earlier this year had already set off the alarm bells. According to RMS, breakfast demand in January and February was downright gloomy.

Furthermore, if even breakfast-heavy QSRs and value-driven family diners like Wendy’s (WEN) and Denny’s (DENN) stumbled into Q1, how did we expect First Watch Restaurant Group (NASDAQ:FWRG), a pure-play daytime concept that doesn’t have affordable price points?

The hit was coming—and sure enough, it landed.

It was a double-miss, one that hurt. EPS of -$0.01 (misses Wall St. estimates by $0.04) and revenue of $282.3 million (misses Wall St. estimates by $1.08 million). Note that the estimates weren't expecting explosive sales, but what really caught them by surprise was EPS, which was $0.12 in Q1.

The release of these results caused the stock to plummet nearly 30% to below $14 - my first point of interest. The stock was previously trading above $18, which is about 12.5% ​​above my PT of $16, which I considered and still consider to be overvalued territory for this stock.

But before we dive into these numbers in full, let’s take a look at the daytime dining landscape in the United States for the entire Q1. In my last First Watch write-up, we only had some data for the first few months, but we have the full data on the table now.

If you’re a reader of Goulart’s Restaurant Stocks or an industry enthusiast, you know that breakfast is the most discretionary meal of all. When guests aren’t looking to spend their hard-earned dollars, it’s the first time that suffers from a lack of traffic.

You can’t get around this rule, but you can alleviate it by offering promotions or items at lower price points, as Wendy’s, Denny’s, IHOP, and even c-stores do. This usually keeps some traffic, but not enough for the segment to outperform other occasions like lunch, and especially dinner.

Imagine First Watch with me. High price points, more elaborate food (almost like fine dining for breakfast) and few promotions.

This would never work in a value-driven environment with discretionary spending cuts.

Let’s take a look at Revenue Management Solutions’ trends for breakfast month-over-month so far:

  • January: Traffic fell 0.2% year-over-year, with breakfast traffic down 6.9% year-over-year after a near double-digit drop in December;
  • February: Traffic down 2.8% year-over-year, with breakfast traffic down 12.6% year-over-year;
  • Q1 Consolidated: Traffic down 0.7% year-over-year, with breakfast traffic down 9.5% year-over-year.

This decline in consolidated Q1 traffic contrasts with the 1.7% year-over-year decline in lunch and 2.3% year-over-year growth in dinner.

What we’ve seen in other results shows this breakfast drag in action. Denny’s, for example, saw same-store sales fall 0.9% year over year for franchised locations and 3.2% year over year for company-operated locations. Meanwhile, Wendy’s—which until now has relied on the Biggie Bag and breakfast to drive traffic—saw its same-store sales fall 2.1% year over year as morning traffic declined.

A few question marks like Tim Hortons (QSR) will tell us how things are going on the upper side of the border—and whether there’s any way to stem this with a strong value-per-dollar equation.

Looking at First Watch, revenue grew 16.4% year-over-year in Q1 to $282.3 million.

This growth was driven by the opening of twelve net restaurants in the quarter system-wide (bringing the restaurant count to 584 restaurants nationwide) and same-store sales growing 0.7% year-over-year.

If you look back, you might think that same-store sales weren’t that bad, right? You might be right, but you’d need to consider traffic trends. First Watch is facing increasingly easy traffic comparisons—in Q1 FY 2024 traffic plunged 4.5% year-over-year.

Just take a look at same-store sales compared to other restaurants:

Breaking down same-store sales, we have the following scenario:

  • Traffic down 0.7% year-over-year. It had already fallen for at least eight consecutive quarters, accelerating the decline in FY 2024 when it reached mid-single digits year-over-year;
  • Average check per guest growth was 1.4% year over year. It was growing in the high single digits year over year in FY 2023, slowing to mid-to-low single digits in FY 2024 and reaching low single digits now. There is clearly resistance to raising prices much further and continuing to drive down traffic. The mix isn’t helping much either, as guests are likely compressing their orders and/or choosing some of the cheaper menu items.

Observe how this relationship occurred over the quarters:

But that still doesn't explain the drop in profitability. We'll have to dig deeper.

In Q1 FY 2025 restaurant-level margins plummeted 430 bp year-over-year to 16.5%. This was perhaps the biggest headwind of the quarter. To a lesser extent, AUV has been declining since FY 2023 ($2.25 million in FY 2023 vs. $2.2 million in FY 2024).

But coming back to restaurant-level margins, what were the drivers of the decline? We need to look at the cost centers:

  • Food, Beverage and Packaging accounted for 23.8% of revenue in the quarter. That was a substantial increase from 21.8% in the same period last year. One of the biggest contributors was the cost of eggs, which are trading at an all-time high. Of course, First Watch buys them wholesale through contracts, which helps control the price hike. However, prices continue to rise, reaching over $6 at retail and are expected to continue the momentum as tariffs make them even more expensive;
  • Labor costs accounted for about 34.6% of revenue in Q1 FY 2025. Again, this represents a 130 bp increase year over year. This increase is interesting given that turnover has decreased, and the company has implemented a new app-based scheduling system. This has brought some tailwinds thus far, but First Watch has managed to offset wage inflation in the period;
  • Occupancy costs were 8.3% of revenue, growing 30 bp year-on-year.

At the corporate level, G&A fell 80 bp year-over-year relative to revenue and now represents 10.7%.

This partially offset, but certainly did not make much of a difference to the bottom line.

EBITDA was $18.35 million in Q1 FY 2024, down about 26.23% year-over-year—a margin of about 6.5%. This is much lower than the company’s 5-year average (9.13%) and last year’s average (10.03%).

After interest on debt, net income of $7.2 million turned into a loss of $829,000. Quite a drop.

Taking all this into consideration, I still see headwinds going forward, and I'll tell you why:

  1. Food costs are expected to continue to be a headwind. The USDA forecasts a 41% increase in egg prices in 2025, with prices expected to remain high in 2026 depending on supply recovery and continued tariffs. Dairy—another important First Watch product category—is also expected to remain high in 2025, with some adjustments to account for the impact of tariffs (although the USDA has lowered some projections for cheddar cheese, butter, and powdered milk). Animal protein is also expected to be high in 2025;
  2. Since First Watch has been hiking menu prices pretty aggressively for a while now, there’s not much wiggle room left. That makes it tough to offset the dip in traffic—and the result is clear: slimmer restaurant-level margins;
  3. Labor inflation is still high, but is expected to remain elevated in the mid- to high single digits;
  4. To get AUVs back up and running and dilute fixed costs, First Watch will need to grow same-restaurant sales again. To do that, they will need to drive traffic (as average checks per guest are already stretched), and that’s tough in an environment where daytime dining is weaker than ever.

Guidance Changed: Adjusted EBITDA Expected to Be Lower

Due to trends observed thus far in Q1 FY 2025, First Watch has reduced its adjusted EBITDA target from $124 million to $130 million to $114 million to $119 million for the full FY 2025. This is a decrease of approximately 8% to 8.5%.

Everything else is business as usual: sales up 20% year over year, same-store sales flat to low single digits, 59 to 64 new openings (offset by 3 closures), and Capex still projected between $150 million and $160 million.

After Stocks Plunge, Is It a Buy?

As I type this, First Watch is sitting at $15.50 a share. I definitely wouldn't consider this stock a bargain. After all, it's trading at just $0.50 (or 3.22%) off my $16 PT.

We could think about scooping some up under $14, maybe right after the opening bell—but right now? Not the time.

However, let's not be so simplistic. Let's take a look at some multiples that I consider important.

Considering that EBITDA is about 10% to 14% lower than adjusted EBITDA, we should see (as per guidance, of course) an EBITDA of $102 million. With an EV of $1.91 billion, the stock would be trading at an EV/EBITDA of approximately 18.72x.

This is about 97.88% higher than the industry median for projected EV/EBITDA for FY 2025. Sure, First Watch is growing faster than the industry average. But should we pay a premium for a daytime-only restaurant when daytime sales are down?

I don't think so.

Portillo's (PTLO), Shake Shack (SHAK), Kura Sushi (KRUS), and even Dutch Bros (BROS) are growth plays that I find more appetizing than First Watch near $16.

That said, First Watch still looks like a 'Hold' here. Sure, the stock could break through my $16 PT and trade up to $18 at a premium, like it did before earnings. However, if it hits that point again and daytime trading doesn't improve for Q2, the stock will fall again.

If you want to get in here, I see a suitable price below $14 or, at best, closer to $12.
